_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 1-8 Behavioral Health Emergencies (Revised 10/19/2022, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan General Treatment Protocol Section 1-8: Behavioral Health Emergencies — MDHHS Approved 10/19/22, Reviewed 2023

sections.title: Behavioral Health Emergencies
points: Respect the dignity of the patient
Treat known conditions such as hypoglycemia, hypoxia, or poisoning — refer to appropriate protocol

Patients experiencing behavioral health emergencies should be transported for treatment if they: can be reasonably expected to intentionally or unintentionally physically injure themselves or others (or has made threats); are unable to attend to basic physical needs; have judgement so impaired they are unable to understand the need for treatment and whose behavior will cause significant physical harm; have weakened mental processes due to age, epilepsy, alcohol or drug dependence which impairs treatment decisions
Communicate in a calm and nonthreatening manner — be conscious of personal body language and tone of voice
Keep contacts to a minimum; when prudent, utilize a single rescuer for assessment
Offer your assistance to the patient
Constantly monitor and observe patient to prevent injury or harm
Control environmental factors; attempt to move patient to a private area — maintain escape route
Attempt de-escalation using an empathetic approach — avoid confrontation
If patient becomes violent or actions present a threat to patient's safety or that of others, restraint may be necessary — refer to Patient Restraint-Procedure Protocol

If patient is severely agitated, combative/aggressive, and shows signs of sweating, delirium, elevated temperature, and lack of fatiguing — refer to Hyperactive Delirium Syndrome with Severe Agitation-Treatment Protocol

sections.title: Protective Custody (Michigan Mental Health Code Reference)
points: Protective Custody: the temporary custody of an individual by a law enforcement officer — with or without the individual's consent — for the purpose of protecting that individual's health and safety, or the health and safety of the public, and for the purpose of transporting the individual if the individual appears, in the judgment of the law enforcement officer, to be a person requiring treatment
Protective custody is civil in nature and is not to be construed as an arrest (330.1100c(7), Sec. 100c, Michigan Mental Health Code)

deterioration_flags: Severe agitation with signs of sweating, delirium, elevated temperature, and lack of fatiguing — refer to Hyperactive Delirium Syndrome with Severe Agitation protocol
Violence or physical threat to crew or patient — consider restraint per Patient Restraint-Procedure Protocol; ensure scene safety
Underlying medical cause suspected (hypoglycemia, hypoxia, poisoning) — treat underlying condition per appropriate protocol before attributing behavior to psychiatric cause
scope_notes: Patient restraint: refer to Patient Restraint-Procedure Protocol
Hyperactive delirium management: refer to Hyperactive Delirium Syndrome-Treatment Protocol
Sedation: Specialist skill — refer to applicable protocol
Protective custody: law enforcement function — not EMS authority
